- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.

import { StrictMode } from 'react';
import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client';
import { BrowserRouter, Navigate, Route, Routes } from 'react-router-dom';
import {
  ActivationPendingPage,
  CodexPage,
  DashboardPage,
  DemoPage,
  HomePage,
  PrivacyPage,
  PricingPage,
  TermsPage,
  VicinityPage,
  VantagePage,
} from './App.tsx';
import './index.css';

createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render(
  <StrictMode>
    <BrowserRouter>
      <Routes>
        <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
        <Route path="/demo" element={<DemoPage />} />
        <Route path="/codex" element={<CodexPage />} />
        <Route path="/vantage" element={<VantagePage />} />
        <Route path="/vicinity" element={<VicinityPage />} />
        <Route path="/pricing" element={<PricingPage />} />
        <Route path="/dashboard" element={<DashboardPage />} />
        <Route path="/activation-pending" element={<ActivationPendingPage />} />
        <Route path="/privacy" element={<PrivacyPage />} />
        <Route path="/terms" element={<TermsPage />} />
        <Route path="/app" element={<Navigate to="/demo" replace />} />
        <Route path="*" element={<Navigate to="/" replace />} />
      </Routes>
    </BrowserRouter>
  </StrictMode>,
);

Use an 8px base unit. All spacing is a multiple of 8.

**12-column grid** at all breakpoints. Gutter: 24px desktop / 16px mobile.

|---|---|---|

## 5. LOGO DIRECTION

### Concept

JobFilter is infrastructure for filtering signal from noise — qualified leads surfaced, unqualified suppressed. The mark should communicate a data pipeline or filter architecture, not a hardhat or wrench.

### Mark Description

A vertical three-bar filter icon. Three horizontal bars of descending width (top: 100%, middle: 66%, bottom: 33%), spaced 4px apart, with rounded ends (2px radius). The bars align left, creating a tapering cascade that reads as a funnel rotated 90 degrees — a filter, not a megaphone.

Color: bars are solid `#2563EB`. On dark: `#F1F5F9` bars with `#2563EB` glow (optional).

The mark at 32px height should be clean and legible. No gradients on the mark.

### Wordmark Style

- Font: Inter, weight 700
- Casing: `Job` normal case, `Filter` normal case — one word: `JobFilter`
- Color: `#F1F5F9`
- Letter-spacing: -0.02em
- Position: mark sits 10px left of wordmark, vertically centered
- Optional sub-brand: add `Intake` or `Codex` in 12px, weight 500, `#06B6D4`, beneath and right-aligned to wordmark end — for product-specific contexts

### What to Avoid

- No flag imagery
- No hard hats, hammers, gears, or construction silhouettes
- No rounded-font wordmarks (Nunito-style)
- No gradient wordmarks
- No letter-boxing or enclosing the mark in a shape

## SYNTHESIS

---

### What patterns do the real-feeling ones share that JobFilter lacks?

- **Specific operating metrics, not vague claims.** Rentman has 200+ Capterra reviews. SiteConnect has "3M+ digital sign-ins." BuildScout has "200x ROI." JobFilter has no numbers of any kind that prove the product exists and works.
- **Third-party validation scores.** G2, Capterra, ISO certifications. None of these are self-issued. JobFilter has no third-party credibility anchors.
- **Product visuals.** Every credible site shows the actual interface — screenshots, mockups, animated UI. Rentman, myramsapp, framework.construction, constructionai.io all do this. JobFilter shows nothing.
- **Named, attributable customers.** Not just "a roofing company." A named company, a logo, a person's job title. The real ones use full names. JobFilter has none visible.
- **A single, clear primary CTA.** "Start free trial" or "Book a demo" — one action, one audience. JobFilter's navigation mixes two entirely different products (Codex B2B, Intake Engine B2C) creating decision paralysis.
- **Calm, professional copy.** Framework.construction's "Ask your project anything" outperforms any all-caps screaming headline. The confident ones whisper. The insecure ones shout.

---

### What does demo.zypheragents.co.uk do better than a typical lead-gen site?

- **Cannot be fully assessed** — the site was unreachable across all fetch attempts. However, from the domain structure alone:
- A `/demo` subdomain as the primary public face signals "show, don't sell" — the product IS the landing page
- This approach forces the product to speak for itself rather than marketing copy doing the work
- It implies the product is confident enough to be seen before the sale closes
- It eliminates the "I wonder what it looks like" friction point entirely
- If functioning, it would be the single most credible format in this list because it skips all the trust-building theatre and just lets you use the thing

---

### 5 specific things JobFilter must copy to feel like a real product

1. **Add real UI screenshots to the hero section.** Not mockups. Not illustrations. The actual interface — a job list, a lead card, a filter result. Frame it in a browser or phone device mockup like myramsapp does. This is non-negotiable. No screenshot = no product in the visitor's mind.

2. **Publish one hard metric with a source.** Pick one: number of active users, number of jobs filtered, hours saved per week, or total lead value generated. Put it above the fold. Make it specific ("2,847 tradespeople" beats "thousands of users"). Model this on SiteConnect's operating stats approach.

3. **Separate Codex and Intake Engine into distinct landing pages with distinct URLs and distinct navigation.** Do not mix a B2B manufacturer data product with a B2C trades app on the same nav bar. This is the single biggest credibility killer — it tells every visitor that the company doesn't know who it's talking to. Quickbase handles this cleanly with industry-specific solution pages.

4. **Add one external review badge.** Get 10 reviews on Capterra or Trustpilot and put the widget on the homepage. It doesn't need to be 200 reviews like Rentman — even a 4.8/5 from 12 reviews is a third-party signal that the product is real and people have used it. Self-written testimonials with no verification link are ignored.

5. **Replace at least one all-caps headline with a benefit-led sentence in normal case.** "Ask your project anything" (framework.construction) and "Deliver unforgettable events" (rentman.io) communicate more in a whisper than "STOP MISSING LEADS" does in a scream. Pick the single most painful trade problem JobFilter solves and state it calmly, specifically, and in plain English.

---

### 3 things JobFilter must avoid

1. **Mixing product audiences in the same navigation.** Codex (B2B, manufacturers) and Intake Engine (B2C, tradespeople) are different products with different buyers, different pain points, and different sales motions. Putting them in the same nav bar makes both feel less real, not more comprehensive. Quickbase uses industry-specific solution pages precisely to avoid this confusion.

2. **Using aggressive aesthetics as a substitute for product evidence.** Black and yellow, all-caps, construction-site visual language — this can work as an aesthetic wrapper, but it must wrap something. Rentman uses dark backgrounds and orange CTAs and still feels premium because there are screenshots, ratings, and logos behind the design. JobFilter's aesthetic currently signals "attitude" where "proof" is needed. Aggression without evidence reads as compensation.

3. **A demo page that looks like a developer API console.** If the demo experience requires technical literacy to interpret, it is not a demo — it is a barrier. BuildScout's planning data examples, myramsapp's three-step mobile mockup, and framework.construction's chat interface screenshots all communicate the product to a non-technical buyer in under 10 seconds. A dev-tool-looking demo actively repels the tradespeople who are supposed to be the B2C audience.

---

### How do the best ones handle dual audiences (B2B + B2C)?

- **Quickbase:** Separate solution pages per industry vertical — construction, manufacturing, government, solar. The homepage headline is generic enough to not exclude anyone ("Centralize Work In One Place") but navigation routes each segment to a tailored page. The dual CTA ("Start free trial" for self-serve / "Get a demo" for enterprise) cleanly splits the two buying motions.
- **SiteConnect:** Primary audience is clearly B2B (construction companies, contractors) but the scale metrics ("81,000+ workers protected") speak to the individual worker. The messaging layers: company signs up, workers benefit. This creates a B2B sale with a B2C emotional hook without confusing the two.
- **Rentman:** Strictly single audience (event/media production companies) — avoids the dual-audience problem entirely by being specific about who the product is for. Lesson: being narrow is a feature, not a limitation.
- **The pattern:** The best ones either (a) use separate navigable pages per segment, or (b) choose one primary audience and accept that the other is secondary. None of the credible sites try to serve two fundamentally different buyers from the same hero section. JobFilter needs a hard decision: either build a proper product family architecture (separate /codex and /intake sub-sites with shared branding) or pick one product as the public face and bury the other in a "for manufacturers" link in the footer.

---

**Bottom line for JobFilter:** The aesthetic has edge. The products may be real. But the website currently provides zero evidence that either product exists, works, or has been used by anyone. Every credible site on this list proves existence before asking for trust. JobFilter asks for trust before proving existence. That is the entire gap.</result>

## 2. TYPOGRAPHY SYSTEM

### Font Stack

**Primary:** `Inter` (Google Fonts)
**Mono (data, IDs, codes):** `JetBrains Mono` (Google Fonts)

```
Import: Inter weights 400, 500, 600, 700
Import: JetBrains Mono weights 400, 500
```

Inter reads as infrastructure-grade. Used by Linear, Vercel, Stripe. Zero construction-site associations.

### Type Scale

| Token | Size | Weight | Line-height | Tracking | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | 48px / 3rem | 700 | 1.15 | -0.02em | Hero, page titles |
| H2 | 32px / 2rem | 700 | 1.2 | -0.015em | Section headers |
| H3 | 22px / 1.375rem | 600 | 1.3 | -0.01em | Card titles, feature headers |
| H4 | 18px / 1.125rem | 600 | 1.35 | -0.005em | Sub-sections, table headers |
| Body large | 16px / 1rem | 400 | 1.6 | 0 | Primary body copy |
| Body default | 14px / 0.875rem | 400 | 1.55 | 0 | Card body, descriptions |
| Caption | 12px / 0.75rem | 500 | 1.4 | 0.01em | Labels, metadata, timestamps |
| Mono data | 13px / 0.8125rem | 500 | 1.5 | 0 | Lead IDs, postcode, ref numbers |

### ALL CAPS Rules

**Allowed:**
- Navigation labels (max 4 chars: HOME, DOCS)
- Badge/tag text only (e.g., HOT, NEW, PRO)
- Table column headers paired with tracking: `0.08em`
- Section eyebrows above H2 (e.g., INTAKE ENGINE)

**Banned:**
- Hero headlines
- Button labels
- Card titles
- Body copy of any length
- Pricing plans
- Feature descriptions
- CTA text

Rule: if it is longer than 12 characters, all-caps is banned unconditionally.

## 3. COMPONENT RULES

### Card Style

**Lead cards (Intake Engine)**

- Background: `#111827` (surface)
- Border: 1px solid `#2D3B4F`
- Border-radius: 10px
- Padding: 20px 24px
- Left accent bar: 3px solid, color matches urgency status
- Box-shadow: `0 1px 3px rgba(0,0,0,0.4), 0 0 0 0 transparent`
- Hover state: border-color lifts to `#2563EB`, shadow becomes `0 4px 16px rgba(37,99,235,0.15)`
- Structure (top to bottom):
  - Row 1: Trade category badge (left) + timestamp (right, muted)
  - Row 2: Job title in H4
  - Row 3: Location (postcode + borough) in mono caption + distance pill
  - Row 4: One-line description in Body default
  - Row 5: Three data chips (budget, lead age, source) + action button right-aligned
- No full-bleed images. No decorative icons larger than 16px.

**Feature cards (marketing sections)**

- Background: `#1E2A3A` (elevated surface)
- Border: 1px solid `#2D3B4F`
- Border-radius: 12px
- Padding: 28px 28px
- Icon: 32x32px, colored `#2563EB` on `#2563EB14` background, border-radius: 8px
- Title in H3, body in Body default, no bullets — flowing prose max 2 lines
- No drop shadows on feature cards — border does the work

**Pricing cards**

## 4. LAYOUT PRINCIPLES

### Max Content Width

| Context | Max-width |
|---|---|
| Global container | 1280px |
| Text/prose sections | 720px |
| Dashboard / data | 1440px |
| Modals | 560px |
| Narrow forms | 480px |

Always centered with `margin: 0 auto` and `padding: 0 24px` (mobile) / `0 48px` (tablet) / `0 64px` (desktop).

### Section Spacing Rhythm

Use an 8px base unit. All spacing is a multiple of 8.

| Token | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| space-1 | 4px | Inline micro gaps |
| space-2 | 8px | Component internal tight |
| space-3 | 12px | Component internal default |
| space-4 | 16px | Between related elements |
| space-6 | 24px | Card padding, group gaps |
| space-8 | 32px | Subsection gaps |
| space-12 | 48px | Section internal top/bottom padding |
| space-16 | 64px | Between major sections (mobile) |
| space-24 | 96px | Between major sections (desktop) |
| space-32 | 128px | Hero padding top |

### Grid System

**12-column grid** at all breakpoints. Gutter: 24px desktop / 16px mobile.

| Breakpoint | Columns in use | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Mobile (< 768px) | 1 col (12/12) | Single column, full width |
| Tablet (768–1024px) | 2 col (6/6) | Cards go 2-up |
| Desktop (1025–1280px) | 3 col (4/4/4) | Cards go 3-up |
| Wide (> 1280px) | 4 col (3/3/3/3) | Lead card grid goes 4-up |

Feature sections: 2-col (5 col text + 7 col visual) on desktop. Flip order on alternating rows.
Pricing: Never more than 3 columns. Always centered within max 960px.

### Mobile-First Rules

- Design and code mobile first. Desktop is a progressive enhancement.
- Touch targets: minimum 44x44px for all interactive elements.
- No hover-only states that hide information (tooltips are a hover supplement, not the only source).
- Font sizes never below 14px on mobile body, 12px on captions.
- Cards stack to 100% width below 768px.
- Navigation collapses to drawer below 768px.
- Data tables become horizontally scrollable cards below 768px — do not truncate columns.
- Hero H1 scales: 48px desktop / 36px tablet / 28px mobile.

## 5. LOGO DIRECTION

### Concept

JobFilter is infrastructure for filtering signal from noise — qualified leads surfaced, unqualified suppressed. The mark should communicate a data pipeline or filter architecture, not a hardhat or wrench.

### Mark Description

A vertical three-bar filter icon. Three horizontal bars of descending width (top: 100%, middle: 66%, bottom: 33%), spaced 4px apart, with rounded ends (2px radius). The bars align left, creating a tapering cascade that reads as a funnel rotated 90 degrees — a filter, not a megaphone.

Color: bars are solid `#2563EB`. On dark: `#F1F5F9` bars with `#2563EB` glow (optional).

The mark at 32px height should be clean and legible. No gradients on the mark.

### Wordmark Style

- Font: Inter, weight 700
- Casing: `Job` normal case, `Filter` normal case — one word: `JobFilter`
- Color: `#F1F5F9`
- Letter-spacing: -0.02em
- Position: mark sits 10px left of wordmark, vertically centered
- Optional sub-brand: add `Intake` or `Codex` in 12px, weight 500, `#06B6D4`, beneath and right-aligned to wordmark end — for product-specific contexts

### What to Avoid

- No flag imagery
- No hard hats, hammers, gears, or construction silhouettes
- No rounded-font wordmarks (Nunito-style)
- No gradient wordmarks
- No letter-boxing or enclosing the mark in a shape
